prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old woman.
 
 There was blood in my urine after the marathon--I'm worried I damaged my kidneys from pushing too hard.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a urine test after my annual physical. I ran a half-marathon the day before the test. I feel completely fine--no pain with urinating, no back pain, no fever, and no visible blood in the urine.
 - History: No known kidney problems.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 64
 - Exam: No exam or imaging done.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Urinalysis RBC: 8-10 /hpf (ref 0-2) HIGH
 - Urinalysis blood: Moderate (ref Negative) ABNORMAL
 - Urinalysis WBC: 0-2 /hpf (ref 0-5) normal
 - Urinalysis protein: Negative (ref Negative) normal
 - Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (0.7-1.3) normal
  I can't take time off work right now--I'll lose my job.
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
The blood in your urine is most likely a temporary and harmless condition called exercise-induced hematuria, caused by running the half-marathon. Strenuous exercise can cause small amounts of bleeding into the urine, which usually resolves on its own within a few days. The most important and reassuring parts of your results are that your kidney function test (creatinine) is normal and you have no protein in your urine. This makes significant kidney damage very unlikely. While this is the most probable cause, blood in the urine should be followed up on. The standard approach is to repeat the urine test after a few days of rest from intense exercise to make sure it has cleared.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
The blood in your urine is very likely caused by the half-marathon you ran. This is a common and temporary condition known as "exercise-induced hematuria." Strenuous exercise can cause microscopic bleeding in the urinary tract, which usually resolves on its own within a few days of rest.

The most important results here are your normal creatinine and lack of protein in the urine. These are strong indicators that your kidney function is normal and you have not caused any significant damage. The absence of white blood cells also makes an infection unlikely.

The standard approach is to repeat the urine test after avoiding strenuous exercise for at least 48-72 hours to ensure the blood has cleared.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%
